model_overview.title: Model Overview
model_overview.description: Get a high-level view of the model showing subsystem hierarchy, interfaces, and connections. Use this when first encountering a model to understand the big picture before diving into details.

scope.description: Focus on specific subsystem. Use 'root' for entire model or a block ID like 'blk_10' for a specific subsystem

detail.description: Level of detail: 'tree' (hierarchy only) or 'interfaces' (hierarchy + port names) or 'full' (hierarchy + interfaces + connections)

model_read.title: Model Read
model_read.description:
Inspect the structure and algorithmic behavior of a Simulink model. Returns block topology, connections, and expressions showing what each block does.

Expression notation: uN(source) = input port N with source block.port in parentheses; yN = output port N; @Param(value) = parameter with actual set-value from the block. Example: y1 = @Gain(Kp) * u1(blk_5.y1) means output 1 = Gain parameter times input 1 (from block 5's output 1). When value is numeric (e.g., @Gain(5000)), you have the final value. When value is a variable reference (e.g., @Gain(Kp)), use model_resolve_params to get the numeric value.

Model Reference, Subsystem Reference, and Library blocks show interfaces only, not internal content.

scope.description: Starting scope - use 'root' for model top level or a block ID like 'blk_16'

depth.description: Hierarchy depth limit as string. '0'=current level only, '1'=one level down, 'inf'=full hierarchy

model_query_params.title: Model Query Params
model_query_params.description:
Provides random access to any Simulink parameter on blocks, signals, or model configuration. Complements model_read by exposing values not included in the model graph.

Use when you need:
- Parameters not in model_read expressions (sample times, state names, port dimensions)
- Model configuration (Solver, StopTime, RelTol, code generation options)
- Signal properties beyond names (DataLogging, OutDataTypeStr, Min, Max)
- Targeted verification after model_configure without re-reading the full graph

If model_read already shows the value in an expression (e.g., @Gain(Kp)), you have the set-value. Use model_resolve_params to resolve variable references to numeric values.

Use compile=true only if certain the parameter requires compilation (e.g., CompiledPortWidths). If unsure, use default; PARAM_UNAVAILABLE error will indicate if compile is needed.

targets.description: JSON array of target IDs - blocks (blk_5), signals (blk_5.y1 -> blk_8.u1), or config (config:ModelName)

params.description: JSON array of param names like '["Gain", "SampleTime"]' or '["all"]' for all parameters

compile.description: Compile model before querying ('true' or 'false'). Required for compiled params like CompiledPortWidths

model_resolve_params.title: Model Resolve Params
model_resolve_params.description:
Resolve variable references from model_read expressions to actual numeric values. When model_read shows @Param(Kp), this tool returns the value of Kp from MATLAB workspaces.

Pass expressions exactly as seen in model_read (e.g., "Kp", "cfg.gains.Ki", "TableData(1,:)"). Searches base workspace, model workspace, mask workspaces, and data dictionaries.

Results organized by workspace source; shadowed variables include users list to identify which blocks use each definition.

Unsupported: arithmetic (Kp+Ki), function calls (sqrt(2)), matrix literals ([1 2 3]). Use evaluate_matlab_code for these.

expressions.description: JSON array of expressions to resolve, e.g. '["Kp", "cfg.gains.Ki"]'

model_edit.title: Model Edit
model_edit.description:
Make structural changes or configure parameters in a Simulink model within ONE scope per call. Use model_read first to get block IDs.

Operations (executed in array order):
- add_block: {op, type, name?, ref?, params?, position?} - Add block; params are Simulink block parameters (e.g. {Gain: "2.0"})
- connect: {op, target: "blk_X.y1 -> blk_Y.u1", params?} - Wire output to input (y=output port, u=input port); replaces existing connection if destination port occupied; params set signal properties
- disconnect: {op, target: "blk_X.y1 -> blk_Y.u1"} - Remove connection
- configure: {op, target, params} - Set Simulink parameters. Target types: blk_X (block params, e.g. {Gain: "Kp"}), blk_X.y1 -> blk_Y.u1 (signal props, e.g. {Name: "error"}), or config:ModelName (solver/model settings, e.g. {StopTime: "10"})
- delete: {op, target: "blk_X"} - Remove block
- replace_block: {op, target: "blk_X", type, ref?, params?} - Replace block with different type; preserves name, position, connections
- create_subsystem: {op, name, ref?, interface: {inputs, outputs}} - Create subsystem; connect via #ref.yN/#ref.uN (N = port number from interface)

For parameter-only changes (no structural edits), use configure operations alone.

Chaining: Use "ref" to name a new block, "#ref" to reference it later:
  [{op: "add_block", type: "Gain", ref: "g1"}, {op: "connect", target: "blk_5.y1 -> #g1.u1"}]

Response "created" map shows ref → blk_id. Use model_query_params to discover valid parameter names. Operations continue on error.

IMPORTANT: The operations parameter MUST be single-line JSON with no newlines.

scope.description: Where to operate: 'root' or subsystem block ID like 'blk_5'. All ops must be within this scope.

operations.description: JSON array of operation objects (MUST be single-line, no newlines). Each has 'op' (add_block|connect|disconnect|configure|delete|create_subsystem) and operation-specific fields.

model_test.title: Model Test
model_test.description: Execute behavioral tests against a Simulink model using a Gherkin-format specification file. Auto-creates test artifacts and returns pytest-style results.

gherkin_file.description: Path to Gherkin test specification file

scenarios.description: JSON array of scenario names to run, e.g. '["Scenario1", "Scenario2"]'. Use '[]' to run all scenarios.

verbose.description: Include signal traces on failure ('true' or 'false'). Default: false

draft_mode.description: Skip main model compile for faster iteration ('true' or 'false'). Use 'true' by default for rapid test development (~3s vs ~60s). If draft mode fails due to type/bus/dimension issues, re-run with 'false'. Default: false
